text: The surgeon tests the placement for diaphragm placation stitches, using Tuo Ethibond sutures without pledgets to tighten the diaphragm.

The surgeon uses wristed instruments to place a needle holder precisely on the needle for horizontal mattress interrupted sutures. The first suture is placed in the center of the diaphragm, which is bowed towards the surgeon, aligning future sutures in an anterolateral to posteromedial direction. The diaphragm is manipulated with increased CO₂ pressure.

The surgeon uses wristed instruments to place a horizontal mattress suture on the posterior portion of the diaphragm. The needle is adjusted to achieve the desired angle, and the needle holder provides strong grip strength. The knot is placed on the anterior portion of the diaphragm using a Chitwood knot pusher for efficiency.

The surgeon performs the second horizontal mattress stitch using a backhand suture technique, pulling the needle through the diaphragm with the Cadi Air instrument. The needle holder is used to grasp the diaphragm tissue, leaving visible marks. The surgeon avoids using the needle holder on lung tissue, similar to robotic techniques.
The surgeon uses wristed instruments to roll the needle and achieve precise positioning for sutures, demonstrating the ease of manipulation compared to non-wristed instruments.

The surgeon uses a right-handed instrument to create a ruffle in the diaphragm, ensuring no spleen or stomach is behind it. A nasogastric tube is visible on the left side for stomach decompression. The surgeon ties a Western knot, effectively controlling any minor bleeding from the diaphragm's rich vascular supply.

The surgeon performs an instrument tie using a wristed device, effectively holding the loop and throwing a knot. The device, from Livsmed, facilitates knot tying with ease due to its design. The surgeon completes twelve horizontal mattress sutures, ensuring the diaphragm is tightly placated. The pericardium is visible in the bottom right corner of the surgical field. The surgeon uses a Cavidian EndoStitch for diaphragm placations, shuttling the needle across the tissue.

The surgeon uses wristed instruments to press on the diaphragm, assessing its tightness without CO₂ insufflation. A Maryland bipolar instrument is employed to unroof a cyst located in the mediastinal fat near the phrenic nerve, which is paralyzed. The cyst is visually confirmed not to be a thymoma, and a needle is used to aspirate and decompress it.
